walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Action Board
walkthrough.text: Alright, let's get A Feast for Odin set up! First, unfold the large action board and place it in the center of the table. This is the heart of the game, with sixty-one different action spaces organized into columns. Each column represents a different type of action, and you will be placing your Vikings here throughout the game. Make sure everyone can see and reach it.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Supply Board
walkthrough.text: Next, place the supply board near the action board. This is where all the goods tiles are organized by type and shape. Sort the green, blue, and red tiles onto the supply board in their designated spots. Green tiles are raw goods like beans, flax, and stockfish. Blue tiles are crafted goods like linen, silverware, and clothing. Red tiles are luxury goods like silver, jewelry, and treasure chests.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Goods Tiles
walkthrough.text: Take a moment to familiarize yourself with the goods tiles. They are polyomino shapes, like little Tetris pieces, ranging from single squares up to larger multi-square shapes. Each tile has a value printed on it. Green tiles are worth the least, blue tiles are worth more, and red tiles are the most valuable. Stack each type neatly on the supply board so everyone can grab what they need.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Weapon Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the weapon cards and sort them into four piles by type: bows, snares, spears, and swords. Place each pile face up near the action board. These weapons are used for hunting actions and sometimes for pillaging. You will collect these during the game to improve your hunting success.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Ship Tiles
walkthrough.text: Lay out the ship tiles near the supply board. There are three types: whaling boats, knarrs, and longships. Whaling boats are needed for whaling actions. Knarrs are used for trading and emigration. Longships are used for raiding and pillaging. Sort them by type so players can find what they need quickly.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Exploration Boards
walkthrough.text: Find the exploration boards. These represent islands and territories like Shetland, the Faroe Islands, Iceland, and Greenland. Place them in a row near the main play area where everyone can see them. Each one shows a grid where you can place goods tiles, and they also provide bonus income. You will claim these during the game to expand your placement area.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Occupation Cards
walkthrough.text: Now gather all the occupation cards. There are over one hundred ninety of them, each with a unique ability. Give the deck a thorough shuffle. You will be drawing from this deck during the game. These cards provide special powers and bonuses that can shape your entire strategy.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Home Boards
walkthrough.text: Give each player a home board. This is your personal territory with a large grid on it. The grid has numbers along the top row and along the left side, and each uncovered space at the end of the game is worth negative points. Your main goal is to cover as much of this grid as possible with goods tiles. Place your home board in front of you with plenty of room to work.

walkthrough.title: Give Out Starting Vikings
walkthrough.text: Each player takes their Viking meeples. In a four-player game, everyone starts with a certain number of Vikings based on the rules. These are your workers, and you will place them on the action board to take actions. The number of Vikings you place determines how powerful the action is. One Viking gets you a basic action, while placing three or four gets you something much stronger.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Round Tracker
walkthrough.text: Place the round marker on the first space of the round track. A Feast for Odin is played over seven rounds. Each round has a worker placement phase, a harvest phase, and a feeding phase. The round track also shows when certain bonuses appear and when animals can breed.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Starting Resources
walkthrough.text: Each player receives starting silver and any beginning occupation cards according to the player count rules. Deal each player a hand of occupation cards. Also give each player a starting special tile if the rules call for one. Keep your occupations hidden from other players until you play them.

walkthrough.title: Place Animals and Silver
walkthrough.text: Set up the animal supply near the board. There are cattle, sheep, and pregnant versions of both. Place all the silver coins in a general supply where everyone can reach them. Silver is both a currency and a victory point, so it pulls double duty in this game.

walkthrough.title: Understand Your Home Board Income
walkthrough.text: Take a close look at your home board. See the numbers along each row? The leftmost uncovered column in each row determines your income for that row. As you cover spaces with goods tiles, you uncover higher income values. This income is collected each round during the harvest phase. Covering your board is not just about avoiding negative points. It is also about generating income.

walkthrough.title: Review the Green Tile Rule
walkthrough.text: Here is the most important placement rule in the entire game. Green tiles cannot be placed orthogonally adjacent to other green tiles. That means no green tile can touch another green tile on its top, bottom, left, or right sides. Diagonal is fine, but direct side-to-side contact between green tiles is forbidden. Blue and red tiles have no such restriction. This rule makes covering your board a genuine spatial puzzle.

walkthrough.title: Select First Player
walkthrough.text: Pick someone to go first. In a Feast for Odin, the starting player has a slight advantage because they get first pick of the action spaces. Play will proceed clockwise. The start player may shift between rounds depending on your group's house rules or the official variant you choose. Ready to feast? Let's learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: A Feast for Odin is played over seven rounds. In each round, you take turns placing your Vikings on the action board to gather resources, craft goods, explore new lands, and fill your home board with tiles. After everyone has placed all their Vikings, you go through a harvest phase where you collect income and breed animals, followed by a feeding phase where you must feed all your Vikings. The player with the fewest negative points from uncovered spaces, plus bonuses from silver, exploration, and emigration, wins the game.

walkthrough.title: Worker Placement Basics
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you choose one action space on the board and place one to four Vikings on it. Some actions require just one Viking, while the most powerful actions require three or four. Once an action space is taken, nobody else can use it this round. When you place your Vikings, you immediately resolve the action. Then play passes to the next player. You keep taking turns until everyone has placed all their available Vikings.

walkthrough.title: Understanding Action Columns
walkthrough.text: The action board is organized into columns, and each column gets more powerful as you go down. The top rows in each column cost just one Viking. The middle rows cost two. The bottom rows cost three or four Vikings. Spending more Vikings on a single action gets you a stronger version of that action. For example, a one-Viking hunting action might get you one small tile, while a three-Viking hunt could net you several tiles at once.

walkthrough.title: Gathering Raw Goods
walkthrough.text: Several action columns let you gather raw materials. These include hunting, whaling, fishing, and harvesting from the land. Hunting uses weapon cards to determine your success. You collect green goods tiles like beans, flax, stockfish, and game meat. These raw goods are useful, but remember they are the lowest value tiles and have the adjacency restriction on your board.

walkthrough.title: Hunting with Weapons
walkthrough.text: Hunting actions are special because they use weapon cards. When you hunt, you play weapon cards from your hand to determine how much you catch. Bows, snares, spears, and swords each have different strengths. Before you can hunt effectively, you need to spend actions to acquire weapons. Think of weapon collection as an investment that pays off over multiple hunting trips throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Whaling
walkthrough.text: This is one of the most powerful resource-gathering actions, but it requires four Vikings and a whaling boat. When you go whaling, you can bring home large amounts of goods including whale oil and meat. It is expensive in terms of Vikings and requires preparation, but the payoff can be enormous if you build your strategy around it.

walkthrough.title: Crafting and Upgrading Goods
walkthrough.text: Here is where the real strategy kicks in. You can upgrade green raw goods into blue crafted goods, and blue crafted goods into red luxury goods. Upgrading is critical because blue and red tiles are worth more points, they come in more useful shapes, and most importantly, blue and red tiles have no adjacency restriction. You can place blue and red tiles right next to each other. Upgrading green to blue is one of the most efficient moves in the game.

walkthrough.title: The Polyomino Puzzle
walkthrough.text: Every time you gain a goods tile, you have the option to place it on your home board, on an exploration board you own, or sometimes to set it aside. The tiles are different shapes, like Tetris pieces, and fitting them together is a spatial puzzle. Remember the golden rule: green tiles cannot touch other green tiles on their edges. Blue and red tiles have no such restriction. Plan your placements carefully, because a poorly placed tile can create gaps you can never fill.

walkthrough.title: Home Board Scoring
walkthrough.text: Your home board is the biggest source of both positive and negative points. Every uncovered space at the end of the game costs you points. The numbers printed on the board tell you how much each row and column is worth in income, and how costly uncovered spaces are. Covering your board efficiently is the number one priority in this game. A player who covers their entire board has a massive advantage over someone who leaves gaps.

walkthrough.title: Income from Your Board
walkthrough.text: During the harvest phase, you earn income based on your home board and any exploration boards. For each row on your home board, find the leftmost uncovered column. The number at the top of that column is your income for that row. As you cover more spaces, the uncovered column shifts right, and your income increases. This means every tile you place does double duty: it removes negative points and increases your ongoing income.

walkthrough.title: Exploration
walkthrough.text: Certain actions let you claim exploration boards like Shetland, the Faroe Islands, Iceland, or Greenland. When you explore, you take an exploration board and add it to your play area. These boards give you additional grid space where you can place goods tiles. Each exploration board also has its own income bonuses, making them very valuable. Claiming exploration boards early gives you more rounds to fill them and collect their income.

walkthrough.title: Emigration
walkthrough.text: This is a fascinating strategic option. You can permanently send Vikings away from your workforce. Those Vikings are gone for good, which means you have fewer workers in future rounds, but you never have to feed them again. In return, you receive large one-time bonuses. Emigration requires a knarr and specific goods. It is a bold move that trades future flexibility for immediate rewards and reduced feeding costs.

walkthrough.title: Ships and Their Uses
walkthrough.text: Ships are essential tools in the game. Whaling boats are required for whaling actions. Knarrs enable trading and emigration. Longships are used for raiding and pillaging, which can yield weapons and goods. You acquire ships through specific action spaces. Once you have a ship, it stays with you for the rest of the game unless you use it for emigration.

walkthrough.title: Playing Occupation Cards
walkthrough.text: Occupation cards give you unique ongoing abilities, one-time bonuses, or end-game scoring opportunities. There are specific action spaces for playing occupation cards, and some cost just one Viking while others cost two. The key to occupations is playing them early. An occupation played in round one provides its benefit for the entire game. An occupation played in round six barely helps at all. Look for cards that synergize with your overall strategy.

walkthrough.title: The Harvest Phase
walkthrough.text: After all Vikings are placed, the harvest phase begins. First, collect income from your home board and any exploration boards. Then, check for animal breeding. If you have a pair of sheep, they produce one sheep. If you have a pair of cattle, they produce one cattle. Pregnant animals from previous rounds also give birth. Animals can be placed on your boards as goods tiles or used for food.

walkthrough.title: Animal Breeding
walkthrough.text: Animals follow specific breeding rules. If you have two or more of the same animal type at harvest time, you get one new animal of that type. On the following round, that new animal is pregnant and will produce offspring on its own without needing a pair. Animals are useful both as food for feeding and as tiles to place on your boards. Managing your herds is a balancing act between growing the population and using them as resources.

walkthrough.title: The Feeding Phase
walkthrough.text: After harvest, you must feed your Vikings. Every Viking in your workforce requires one food. Food comes from your goods tiles, animals, or silver coins. If you cannot feed all your Vikings, each unfed Viking costs you three victory points at the end of the game. This is a painful penalty, so always plan ahead. Count your food sources before committing all your Vikings to actions each round.

walkthrough.title: What Counts as Food
walkthrough.text: Many goods can be used as food. Most green goods tiles like beans, grain, and stockfish count as food. Animals can be slaughtered for food. Silver coins each count as one food. Some blue goods also have food value. Check the food value printed on each tile when planning your meals. Silver is particularly flexible because it works as food, currency, and victory points.

walkthrough.title: Silver as Currency
walkthrough.text: Silver plays a special role in the game. Each silver coin is worth one victory point at the end. Silver can also be spent as food during the feeding phase. You earn silver through income, trading, and certain actions. Think of silver as your safety net. It can plug gaps in feeding, but every silver coin you eat is a victory point you lose. Try to find other food sources so your silver stays in your scoring pile.

walkthrough.title: Mountain and Mining Actions
walkthrough.text: Mountain actions let you mine for valuable resources. These actions can yield silver, ore, and sometimes rare goods. Mining is another pathway to filling your board, and the resources can be upgraded just like other goods. Mountain strips are special tiles that can only be placed in certain positions on your board, making them a unique piece of the puzzle.

walkthrough.title: Trading Actions
walkthrough.text: Trading lets you exchange goods for other goods or silver. You need a knarr to trade overseas. Trading is helpful for converting goods you do not need into goods that fill your board better. It is also a way to get blue or red goods without going through the upgrade chain. Efficient trading can save you multiple actions compared to gathering and upgrading separately.

walkthrough.title: Raiding and Pillaging
walkthrough.text: With a longship, you can go raiding or pillaging. These actions let you acquire goods, weapons, and sometimes silver through force. Pillaging uses your weapon cards similar to hunting. Raiding and pillaging can yield excellent rewards, but they require the investment of building a longship first and maintaining a weapon collection.

walkthrough.title: End of Game Scoring
walkthrough.text: After seven rounds, the game ends and you tally your score. First, count the negative points from every uncovered space on your home board and exploration boards. Then add your silver coins, each worth one victory point. Add bonuses from exploration boards and emigration tiles. Add any scoring from occupation cards and English crown bonuses. Subtract three points for each unfed Viking penalty you accumulated. The highest total wins.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Priorities
walkthrough.text: The single biggest factor in scoring is covering your home board. Uncovered spaces can easily cost you twenty to forty points if you are careless. After that, silver accumulation, exploration bonuses, and emigration bonuses are the main positive scoring sources. New players often underestimate how devastating uncovered spaces are. Always prioritize filling your board over chasing flashy actions that leave gaps behind.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your First Feast
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first two rounds with example players. We will cover the essential actions, show you common beginner mistakes, and help you understand the rhythm of the game. Even if you have fewer players at your table, follow along to see how everything works.

walkthrough.title: Starting Position
walkthrough.text: Each player begins with their home board completely uncovered, a handful of Vikings, some starting silver, and a hand of occupation cards. Look at your home board right now. All those empty spaces? Every single one is worth negative points at the end of the game. Your entire mission is to cover them up with goods tiles. Keep that in mind with every action you take.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Acquire a Weapon
walkthrough.text: This costs just one Viking, so it is a small investment. The bow will be used later for hunting actions. Collecting weapons early sets you up for profitable hunts in future rounds. Think of it as sharpening your tools before getting to work.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Harvest Beans
walkthrough.text: They receive a small green tile and place it on their home board. Good start, but remember the green tile rule. That green tile cannot touch another green tile on any of its four edges. Player Two places it in a corner to leave room for future placements.

walkthrough.title: The Green Tile Mistake
walkthrough.text: Now let's say Player Two gets another green tile and tries to place it right next to the first one, edge to edge. That is an illegal move! Green tiles cannot be orthogonally adjacent to other green tiles. This is one of the most common mistakes new players make. You can place them diagonally from each other, but never sharing an edge. If you catch yourself doing this, pick up the tile and find a legal spot.

walkthrough.title: Player Three: Play an Occupation
walkthrough.text: This card gives a bonus every time they take a crafting action for the rest of the game. Playing occupations early is critical. This card will trigger six more times if played in round one, but only once if played in round six. Always consider playing your best occupation card as one of your first moves.

walkthrough.title: The Late Occupation Mistake
walkthrough.text: Here is a mistake we see all the time. A player holds onto a powerful occupation card for three or four rounds, waiting for the perfect moment to play it. Meanwhile, they miss out on three or four rounds of benefits. Unless the card specifically scores at game end, play your occupations as early as possible. The best time to play a round-by-round benefit card is round one.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Go Hunting
walkthrough.text: Using the bow they collected earlier, they succeed at the hunt and gain two green goods tiles. They carefully place these on their home board, making sure no green tile touches another green tile on its edges. Two tiles down, many more to go.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Upgrade Green to Blue
walkthrough.text: They exchange their small green bean tile for a slightly larger blue linen tile. This is a fantastic move. The blue tile is worth more points, it can be placed next to any other tile with no restriction, and it covers more spaces. Upgrading is one of the best things you can do in this game.

walkthrough.title: The Upgrading Lesson
walkthrough.text: Let's compare the strategies. Player Two upgraded and now has a blue tile that covers more board space with no placement restrictions. Player One has two green tiles that cannot touch each other, creating awkward gaps. See the difference? Green tiles fill your board slowly and clumsily. Blue and red tiles fill it efficiently and flexibly. Make upgrading a core part of your strategy from the very start.

walkthrough.title: Player Three: Claim an Exploration Board
walkthrough.text: Player Three places three Vikings to explore and claims the Shetland board. This is a smart early move. Shetland gives them additional grid space to place tiles, plus ongoing income bonuses. The earlier you claim exploration boards, the more rounds you have to fill them and collect their income. Waiting until round five to explore means you only benefit for two rounds.

walkthrough.title: Continuing Round One
walkthrough.text: Play continues with each player placing Vikings one by one. Player One gets more hunting supplies. Player Two gathers resources for another upgrade. Player Three starts filling their new Shetland board. Everyone is building their engine in different ways. When all Vikings are placed, the worker placement phase ends.

walkthrough.title: The Harvest Phase
walkthrough.text: Each player collects income from their home board. Look at each row and find the leftmost uncovered column. The number at the top of that column is your income. Since this is round one, most of your board is still uncovered, so income is low. But notice how Player Two and Player Three, who placed tiles, are already earning slightly more. Every tile you place boosts your income.

walkthrough.title: The Feeding Phase
walkthrough.text: After harvest, you must feed your Vikings. One food per Viking. Player One counts up their Vikings and checks their food supply. They have some beans and stockfish from hunting. Player Two uses a silver coin as food. Remember, silver counts as food but every silver you eat is one victory point lost at the end. Plan your food carefully.

walkthrough.title: The Feeding Mistake
walkthrough.text: Player Three forgot to plan for feeding! They spent all their Vikings on exploration and gathering goods but did not save any food-producing goods. They have to eat three silver coins just to feed their Vikings, losing three victory points. Even worse, if they had no silver, each unfed Viking would cost three victory points as a penalty. Always count your food before committing your last few Vikings to actions.

walkthrough.title: Round Two Begins
walkthrough.text: The action board is cleared. Everyone has a fresh set of actions to choose from. This time, let's see the players apply what they have learned.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Smarter Hunting
walkthrough.text: Player One acquires another weapon and hunts with better results. But this time, instead of placing all the green tiles directly, they upgrade one of them to blue first. They are learning that raw goods are stepping stones, not final products. The blue tile slides neatly onto their board next to the other tiles with no adjacency worries.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Build a Ship
walkthrough.text: This opens up trading and emigration options for the rest of the game. It is a big investment now, but the knarr enables powerful moves in rounds three through seven. Strategic investments in ships pay off over time.

walkthrough.title: Player Three: Fill the Board
walkthrough.text: Player Three focuses on covering spaces, mixing upgraded blue tiles with carefully placed green tiles. They use the Shetland board for extra placement room. By the end of round two, their total uncovered spaces have dropped significantly. This player is on track to minimize negative points, which is the single most important scoring factor in the game.

walkthrough.title: The Board Coverage Lesson
walkthrough.text: Let's compare boards after two rounds. Player Three has the most covered spaces and the highest income. Player Two has fewer tiles but they are mostly blue and well-positioned. Player One has the most tiles but many are green with awkward gaps between them. The lesson is clear: it is not about how many tiles you get. It is about how efficiently they cover your board.

walkthrough.title: Planning Ahead for Feeding
walkthrough.text: This time, all three players plan for feeding before their last action of the round. Player One saves a stockfish for food. Player Two has beans set aside. Player Three learned their lesson and kept a food-producing tile ready. No one has to eat silver this round. Planning ahead is how experienced players avoid the feeding trap.

walkthrough.title: Key Takeaways
walkthrough.text: Let's review what we learned. Cover your home board as the top priority, because uncovered spaces are the biggest source of negative points. Upgrade green goods to blue whenever possible, because blue tiles are more flexible and efficient. Play occupation cards early to maximize their benefit over many rounds. Always plan for feeding before your last actions each round.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have played through two rounds of A Feast for Odin. You have seen worker placement, goods gathering, crafting upgrades, exploration, and the harvest and feeding cycle. You have also learned from three common mistakes: placing green tiles adjacent to each other, holding occupation cards too long, and forgetting to plan for feeding. You are ready to continue on your own.

walkthrough.title: Before You Feast
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and common mistakes to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to goods types, action categories, and game terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Enjoy the feast!

summary.title: Player Three: Play an Occupation
bullets: 1 Viking to play an occupation card with ongoing benefit
Card triggers every crafting action for the rest of the game
Playing early = maximum benefit over more rounds

summary.title: The Late Occupation Mistake
bullets: Holding occupation cards wastes rounds of benefits
Round 1 play = 6 more triggers; Round 6 play = 1 trigger
Play ongoing-benefit occupations ASAP

summary.title: Player One: Go Hunting
bullets: 2 Vikings + bow = successful hunt, 2 green tiles
Place carefully: no green-to-green edge contact
Weapons make hunting more productive

summary.title: Player Two: Upgrade Green to Blue
bullets: 2 Vikings to craft: exchange green tile → blue tile
Blue tile: higher value, larger shape, NO adjacency restriction
One of the best moves in the game

summary.title: The Upgrading Lesson
bullets: Green tiles fill board slowly with awkward gaps
Blue/red tiles fill efficiently with no restrictions
Make upgrading a core strategy from the start

summary.title: Player Three: Claim an Exploration Board
bullets: 3 Vikings to explore → claim Shetland board
Extra grid space + ongoing income bonuses
Claim early for more rounds of benefit

summary.title: Continuing Round One
bullets: Players continue placing Vikings one by one
Each building their strategy: hunting, upgrading, exploring
Round ends when all Vikings are placed

summary.title: The Harvest Phase
bullets: Collect income from home board rows
Leftmost uncovered column = income value
More tiles placed → higher income each round

summary.title: The Feeding Phase
bullets: 1 food per Viking required
Food sources: green goods, animals, silver
Silver as food works but costs you 1 VP each

summary.title: The Feeding Mistake
bullets: Player 3 forgot to save food → must eat 3 silver (-3 VP)
Unfed Vikings cost -3 VP penalty each
Always count food BEFORE committing last Vikings

summary.title: Round Two Begins
bullets: All Vikings return, action board clears
Fresh round with lessons learned
Players apply what they learned from Round 1

summary.title: Player One: Smarter Hunting
bullets: Hunt + upgrade: don't keep everything as green
Raw goods are stepping stones, not final products
Blue tiles fit neatly with no adjacency worries

summary.title: Player Two: Build a Ship
bullets: 3 Vikings to build a knarr
Opens trading + emigration for remaining rounds
Big investment now, strategic payoff later

summary.title: Player Three: Fill the Board
bullets: Mix of upgraded blue tiles + carefully placed green tiles
Use Shetland board for extra placement room
Minimizing uncovered spaces = best scoring strategy

summary.title: The Board Coverage Lesson
bullets: It's not about how many tiles — it's about efficient coverage
Fewer blue tiles > many green tiles with gaps
Best boards have a mix of upgraded tiles tightly packed

summary.title: Planning Ahead for Feeding
bullets: All players save food before last action
No silver eaten this round = more VP preserved
Experienced players always plan feeding first

summary.title: Key Takeaways
bullets: Cover home board = top priority (biggest point swing)
Upgrade green → blue whenever possible
Play occupations early, plan feeding every round

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: Completed 2 practice rounds of A Feast for Odin
Learned from 3 common mistakes: adjacency, late occupations, no food plan
Ready to continue on your own

summary.title: Before You Feast
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix → quick-reference for goods and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we talk strategy, here are the mistakes that cost new players the most points. First, leaving too many uncovered spaces on your home board. This is the number one point drain and the most common beginner trap. Second, placing green tiles next to each other, which is an illegal move that has to be corrected. Third, forgetting to plan feeding each round, leading to negative three victory points per unfed Viking. Fourth, ignoring the upgrade path from green to blue goods, which leaves you with inefficient tiles and adjacency headaches. Fifth, not claiming exploration boards, which means missing out on extra placement area and income. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the biggest pitfalls.

walkthrough.title: Upgrade Everything
walkthrough.text: If there is one tip that separates beginners from experienced players, it is this: upgrade your goods. Green tiles are raw materials, and they should be treated as temporary holdings. As soon as you can, upgrade them to blue. Blue tiles are worth more, have better shapes for filling your board, and most importantly, they have no adjacency restriction. You can pack blue tiles side by side with no gaps. Red tiles are even better, but the jump from green to blue is where the biggest efficiency gain happens. Build upgrading into every round of your game plan.

walkthrough.title: Play Occupations Early
walkthrough.text: Occupation cards are powerful, but only if you play them early enough to benefit from them. An occupation that gives you a bonus every round is worth six bonuses if played in round one, but only one bonus if played in round six. Even occupations that seem modest can add up to massive advantages over the course of a game. Unless a card specifically scores at the end of the game regardless of when it was played, aim to get your occupations on the table in the first two rounds.

walkthrough.title: Always Plan for Feeding
walkthrough.text: Every round, each Viking in your workforce needs one food. This sounds manageable, but it sneaks up on you. Before you commit your last few Vikings to actions each round, count your food sources. Do you have enough beans, stockfish, or animals to cover everyone? Can you use silver as a backup without sacrificing too many victory points? The negative three point penalty for each unfed Viking is brutal. Experienced players plan their food supply before they even start placing Vikings.

walkthrough.title: Explore Early
walkthrough.text: Exploration boards are incredibly valuable for two reasons. First, they give you additional grid space to place tiles, which helps you earn positive points instead of just reducing negatives. Second, they provide income bonuses that compound over the remaining rounds. A Shetland board claimed in round one gives you six rounds of extra income. The same board claimed in round five gives you only two rounds. The math strongly favors exploring early. If you see an exploration action available and you have the Vikings for it, seriously consider taking it.

walkthrough.title: Balance Short-Term and Long-Term
walkthrough.text: Gathering food and small tiles is a short-term need for feeding and basic board coverage. Building ships, collecting weapons, and claiming exploration boards are long-term investments that pay off over multiple rounds. A common mistake is focusing entirely on one side. If you only gather, you fall behind in efficiency. If you only invest, you starve your Vikings. The best players spend the first two rounds building their engine, then shift to production in rounds three through seven.

walkthrough.title: Think About Tile Shapes
walkthrough.text: Not all tiles are equally useful, even at the same value. When choosing between tiles of the same color, look at their shapes. Long, thin pieces are harder to work with because they create odd gaps. Compact squares and rectangles fill space more efficiently. Also consider what shapes your board already needs. Sometimes a lower-value tile with the right shape is more valuable than a higher-value tile that does not fit anywhere useful. Train yourself to think about shape fit before tile value.

walkthrough.title: Silver Is Your Safety Net
walkthrough.text: Silver is the most flexible resource in the game. It counts as one victory point, one food, and can sometimes be spent for other purposes. But that flexibility makes it tempting to overspend. Every silver coin you use for food is one victory point you lose. Try to build a food pipeline from green goods and animals so your silver can stay in your scoring pile. Think of silver as your emergency fund. You want to have it available, but you do not want to spend it unless you absolutely have to.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Leaving uncovered spaces on home board = biggest point drain
Green tiles adjacent to green tiles = illegal move
Forgetting feeding plan = -3 VP per unfed Viking
Ignoring upgrades = inefficient tiles with adjacency problems
Skipping exploration = missing extra space + income

walkthrough.title: The Whaling Engine
walkthrough.text: Whaling is one of the most powerful strategies in the game, but it requires serious commitment. You need to acquire a whaling boat and dedicate four Vikings to the whaling action every round. The payoff is enormous: whaling yields large quantities of valuable goods in a single action. The key is getting your whaling boat by round two at the latest. Once you have it, every round becomes a harvest of whale oil and meat that can be upgraded or placed directly. Build your entire strategy around the whaling action and use your remaining Vikings for upgrades and board coverage.

walkthrough.title: The Upgrade Chain Strategy
walkthrough.text: Instead of chasing quantity, focus entirely on quality. Gather a few green goods early, then spend your actions upgrading them: green to blue, blue to red. Red tiles are the most valuable per square and have the best shapes for filling your board. A single red tile can cover as much board space as two or three green tiles combined, with no adjacency restrictions. The upgrade chain costs actions, but the board efficiency gain is massive. Pair this with occupation cards that reduce upgrade costs or grant bonus upgrades.

walkthrough.title: The Exploration Rush
walkthrough.text: Exploration boards are among the most valuable assets in the game. This strategy prioritizes claiming exploration boards in rounds one and two. Each board gives you extra placement area and ongoing income. With two exploration boards claimed early, you have significantly more space to fill and two extra income sources compounding for five or six rounds. The downside is that exploration actions cost three or four Vikings, leaving you fewer workers for resource gathering. Compensate by using the income from your boards to buy goods through trading actions.

walkthrough.title: The Emigration Strategy
walkthrough.text: Emigration is a bold move that most beginners overlook. By permanently sending Vikings away with a knarr and goods, you receive large one-time bonuses and permanently reduce your feeding burden. Fewer Vikings means fewer mouths to feed, which means you can direct more resources toward board coverage instead of food. The optimal time for emigration is rounds three through five, after you have established your engine but before the late game. Build a knarr early, then emigrate when you have surplus goods you cannot efficiently place.

walkthrough.title: Occupation Card Synergies
walkthrough.text: With over one hundred ninety occupation cards, the variety is staggering. Advanced players learn to spot synergies between their occupation cards and their chosen strategy. Some cards chain together: one card reduces the cost of crafting, another gives a bonus when you craft. Together they make upgrading nearly free. Other cards create entirely new pathways, like letting you trade without a knarr or hunt without weapons. When you draw your starting occupations, look for cards that reinforce each other rather than pulling you in different directions.

walkthrough.title: The Animal Breeding Engine
walkthrough.text: Animals are an underappreciated resource in the game. If you acquire a pair of sheep in round one, they breed every harvest. By round four, you could have a small herd. Animals serve as food, board tiles, and can be placed on exploration boards. The breeding engine is passive, meaning it runs without costing you any actions after the initial acquisition. Pair animal breeding with an exploration board like Iceland that has space for animal placement, and you have a self-sustaining engine that covers spaces and feeds Vikings with minimal action investment.

walkthrough.title: Action Space Denial
walkthrough.text: In multiplayer games, blocking opponents can be just as important as helping yourself. Each action space can only be used once per round. If you see an opponent building toward a whaling strategy, taking the whaling boat action before they do can set them back an entire round. Similarly, occupying the three-Viking hunting space forces opponents to use the weaker one-Viking version. Advanced players pay attention to what their opponents need and time their actions to deny critical spaces while still advancing their own strategy.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Board Optimization
walkthrough.text: In the final two rounds, shift your focus entirely to minimizing uncovered spaces. Every single uncovered square on your home board and exploration boards costs you points. At this stage, the shape of a tile matters more than its value. A one-value tile that fills a gap perfectly is worth more than a three-value tile that leaves spaces open. Count your uncovered squares and work backward from what shapes you need. Sometimes the best move is trading valuable goods just to get a tile shape that completes a section of your board.

walkthrough.title: The Longship Raiding Path
walkthrough.text: Building a longship and investing in weapons opens up raiding and pillaging actions. These actions can yield significant goods and silver, especially at the three and four Viking level. The raiding path works best when combined with early weapon collection. By round three, a well-armed player with a longship can pull in large hauls every round. The risk is the upfront cost: building a longship and collecting weapons takes four to five actions across rounds one and two. If you commit to raiding, make sure the payoff exceeds what you could have earned through simpler gathering strategies.

walkthrough.title: Silver Accumulation Strategy
walkthrough.text: Every silver coin is one victory point. While most strategies treat silver as a byproduct, a dedicated silver strategy makes it the primary scoring mechanism. Use mining actions to generate silver directly. Trade goods for silver when the conversion rate is favorable. Claim exploration boards that provide silver income. The beauty of this strategy is that silver also serves as food, so you never starve. The challenge is that silver does not cover board spaces, so you still need a separate plan for tile placement. Hybrid players often accumulate fifteen to twenty silver while maintaining decent board coverage through upgraded tiles.

entries.definition: Each player's personal territory board with a large grid. Uncovered spaces at game end count as negative points. Covering spaces also increases income from each row. The home board is the primary scoring area.
The large central board with sixty-one action spaces organized into columns. Players place Vikings here to take actions. Each space can only be used once per round.
The board where all goods tiles are organized by type and shape. Players take goods from here when instructed by their actions.
Island and territory boards (Shetland, Faroe, Iceland, Greenland, etc.) that provide additional placement grids and income bonuses. Claimed through exploration actions.
The smallest and easiest exploration board to claim. Provides a small placement grid and modest income bonus. Good first exploration target.
A mid-sized exploration board with a moderate placement grid and income bonus. A solid second exploration target after Shetland.
A large exploration board with significant placement area and good income bonuses. Requires more Vikings to claim but offers strong rewards.
One of the largest and most valuable exploration boards. Offers extensive placement area and high income, but costs many Vikings to claim.

The lowest tier of goods tiles representing raw materials: beans, flax, stockfish, game meat, etc. Green tiles CANNOT be placed orthogonally adjacent to other green tiles. Worth the least points but often easiest to acquire.

The middle tier of goods tiles representing processed materials: linen, clothing, silverware, etc. Blue tiles have NO adjacency restriction and can be placed next to any tile. Created by upgrading green goods.

The highest tier of goods tiles representing luxury items: silver hoard, jewelry, treasure chests, etc. Red tiles have NO adjacency restriction, are the most valuable per square, and have the best shapes. Created by upgrading blue goods.
A small green goods tile. Low value, but counts as food for the feeding phase. One of the most basic resources in the game.
A green goods tile representing raw plant fiber. Can be upgraded to linen (blue). Important step in the crafting chain.
A green goods tile representing dried fish. Counts as food and can be upgraded. Commonly acquired through fishing actions.
A green goods tile acquired through hunting actions. Counts as food and can be upgraded. Requires weapons for efficient hunting.
A green goods tile acquired through whaling. Valuable as a raw material for upgrading. Part of the whaling strategy payoff.
A blue crafted goods tile made by upgrading flax (green). Has no adjacency restriction and better shapes than green tiles.
A blue crafted goods tile. Higher value than raw goods, no adjacency restriction. Can be further upgraded to red luxury goods.
A blue crafted goods tile representing worked silver items. Valuable for board placement and can be upgraded to red tier.
A tile shape made up of connected squares, like Tetris pieces. Goods tiles in A Feast for Odin are polyominoes of various shapes and sizes that must be fitted together on your boards.
Two tiles sharing an edge (top, bottom, left, or right). Green tiles cannot be orthogonally adjacent to other green tiles. Diagonal contact does not count as orthogonal adjacency.
Worker meeples placed on the action board to take actions. Actions cost one to four Vikings depending on power level. Must be fed one food each per round.

The most flexible resource. Each silver coin equals one victory point at game end, can be used as one food during feeding, and serves as currency for certain actions. Earned through income, mining, and trading.
A resource obtained through mining actions. Can be used in crafting and upgrading. Part of the mountain mining pathway.
Required to feed Vikings during the feeding phase (one food per Viking per round). Many green goods, animals, and silver count as food. Unfed Vikings cost three victory points each.
An animal resource that can be used as food or placed on boards as a tile. Sheep breed in pairs at harvest: two sheep produce one new sheep. Pregnant sheep produce offspring alone.
An animal resource that can be used as food or placed on boards as a tile. Cattle breed in pairs at harvest. More valuable than sheep as both food and board tiles.
When animals breed at harvest, the new offspring is marked as pregnant. On the next harvest, pregnant animals produce one offspring on their own without needing a mate.
An action category that uses weapon cards to acquire green goods tiles. Hunting success depends on the weapons you have collected. Multiple strength levels available (one to four Vikings).
A powerful four-Viking action that requires a whaling boat. Yields large quantities of goods including whale oil and meat. One of the highest-payoff single actions in the game.
An action to acquire fish-related green goods tiles. Simpler than hunting (no weapons needed). Yields food-value tiles useful for the feeding phase.
An action to gather agricultural goods like beans and grain. Basic resource-gathering that requires few Vikings. Good for food supply.
An action to extract ore, silver, and other mountain resources. Can yield valuable materials for crafting and direct victory points through silver.

An action that upgrades goods from one tier to the next: green to blue, or blue to red. One of the most efficient actions in the game because upgraded tiles cover more board space with no adjacency restrictions.
An action to claim exploration boards (Shetland, Faroe, Iceland, Greenland, etc.). Costs three to four Vikings but provides extra placement area and ongoing income bonuses.

An action to permanently remove Vikings from your workforce. Requires a knarr and goods. Provides large one-time bonuses and reduces future feeding costs. A strategic trade of flexibility for immediate reward.
An action to exchange goods for other goods or silver. Requires a knarr for overseas trading. Can skip the upgrade chain by acquiring blue or red goods directly.
An action available with a longship. Yields goods and silver. Uses weapon cards for success. Part of the aggressive military strategy path.
An action available with a longship and weapon cards. Similar to raiding but with different rewards. Weapon cards determine the outcome and quality of plunder.
An action to play occupation cards from your hand. Costs one to two Vikings depending on the action space. Cards provide ongoing abilities, one-time bonuses, or end-game scoring.
A ship required for whaling actions. Must be acquired through a ship-building action before you can whale. Stays with you for the rest of the game once built.
A versatile trading ship used for overseas trading and emigration actions. Required to trade goods for other goods or silver overseas, and to emigrate Vikings for bonuses.
A warship used for raiding and pillaging actions. Combined with weapon cards to determine raid success. Enables the aggressive military strategy path.
A weapon card used in hunting and pillaging. One of four weapon types. Collected through weapon acquisition actions on the action board.
A weapon card used for trapping game during hunting actions. One of four weapon types. Useful for catching smaller animals.
A weapon card used in hunting and combat. One of four weapon types. Effective for larger game and raid actions.
A weapon card used primarily in raiding and pillaging. One of four weapon types. Often the most powerful weapon for combat-related actions.
The main phase of each round where players take turns placing Vikings on action spaces. Continues clockwise until all players have placed all their available Vikings.
The phase after worker placement where players collect income from their home board and exploration boards, and animals breed. Income is determined by the leftmost uncovered column in each row.
The final phase of each round. Each Viking requires one food. Food can come from green goods tiles, animals, or silver. Each unfed Viking results in a three victory point penalty.

Resources collected during the harvest phase. Determined by the leftmost uncovered column value in each row of your home board and exploration boards. Covering more spaces shifts the income column right, increasing income.
During harvest, pairs of same-type animals produce one offspring. The new animal is marked as pregnant and will produce another offspring on its own during the next harvest phase.
One of over one hundred ninety unique cards providing special abilities. Can grant ongoing bonuses, one-time effects, or end-game scoring. Playing early maximizes benefit over remaining rounds.
A bonus scoring element found on some tiles and boards. Each English crown is worth additional victory points at the end of the game.

Empty squares on your home board and exploration boards at game end. Each uncovered space subtracts points from your score. The single biggest scoring factor in the game — covering your boards is the top priority.

Points lost due to uncovered spaces on boards and unfed Viking penalties. Uncovered spaces can cost twenty to forty points if neglected. Minimizing negative points is more important than maximizing positive points.
A penalty of three victory points for each Viking you cannot feed during the feeding phase. Applied at the end of the game. Always plan food sources before committing Vikings to actions.
A one-time scoring bonus received when you permanently emigrate Vikings using a knarr and goods. Provides significant victory points in exchange for losing workforce for future rounds.
Victory points earned from claimed exploration boards at game end. Each board has specific bonus values based on how well it is covered with tiles.

Calculated after Round 7: subtract uncovered space penalties, add silver (1 VP each), add exploration bonuses, emigration bonuses, English crown bonuses, and occupation card bonuses. Subtract starvation penalties. Highest total wins.
